features.summary: NVIDIA reported Q2 fiscal 2021 revenue of $3.87 billion, a 50% increase year-on-year and 26% sequentially, driven by strong performance in gaming and data center segments. Gaming revenue reached $1.65 billion, up 26% year-on-year, while data center revenue hit a record $1.75 billion, up 167% year-on-year, bolstered by the Mellanox acquisition and the launch of the Ampere architecture. The company also noted a decline in professional visualization and automotive revenues due to pandemic-related impacts.
features.management_outlook: For Q3 fiscal 2021, NVIDIA expects revenue of approximately $4.4 billion, with gaming anticipated to grow over 25% sequentially and data center revenue projected to increase in the low to mid-single digits. Management highlighted strong visibility in gaming and data center demand, driven by ongoing trends in cloud computing and AI adoption, while also acknowledging challenges in professional visualization and automotive sectors.
features.risks: NVIDIA faces risks related to ongoing uncertainties from the COVID-19 pandemic, which could impact enterprise demand in professional visualization and automotive segments. Additionally, the company is navigating supply chain complexities and potential capacity digestion among hyperscale customers, which may affect data center growth in the near term.
features.key_drivers: Key growth drivers for NVIDIA include the increasing adoption of gaming as a primary entertainment medium, accelerated demand for AI and cloud services, and the successful launch of the Ampere architecture. The integration of Mellanox is enhancing NVIDIA's data center capabilities, while the expansion of GeForce NOW and the introduction of new gaming titles are expected to further boost gaming revenue.
